data.title: WTI crude will near $115/bbl by the end of 2026 – Kalshi
data.description:
Investors on prediction market Kalshi are betting that West Texas Intermediate crude oil (CL1:COM) will surge past $115 per barrel by the end of 2026, with the platform's forecast sitting at $114.68.

According to Kalshi data, traders assign 46.8% odds that oil prices will reach $115.01 or higher before December 31, while the probability of WTI climbing above $120 stands at 38.5% and above $125 at 33.3%.

 

 

 

These bullish bets come as oil markets face mounting geopolitical pressures. As of Thursday’s late morning trade, WTI crude (CL1:COM) hovers around $92 per barrel, while Brent crude (CO1:COM) surpassed $100 for the first time in two months.

The spike followed Iran-backed Houthi militants claiming attacks on two Saudi tankers in the Red Sea's Bab al-Mandeb strait, raising fears that the U.S.-Iran conflict could escalate further.

The Houthi attacks have opened a new front in the conflict by threatening a critical shipping route the Saudis have relied upon during disruptions that largely closed the Strait of Hormuz. When the Bab al-Mandeb Strait is compromised, vessels must detour through the Suez Canal and around Africa, adding 10-15 days to journeys and tying up tanker capacity.

The largest supertankers cannot pass through the Suez when fully loaded, forcing shippers to operate on partial loads and driving up costs for Asian refiners.

Beyond the Red Sea tensions, global oil supplies face additional strain from multiple fronts. Exports from major producer Kazakhstan are endangered by the ongoing Russia-Ukraine war, while worldwide oil inventories have become depleted.

The Wall Street Journal reported that the U.S. is sending additional military forces, medics, and weaponry to the Middle East as President Trump considers expanding operations against Iran—developments that could further roil energy markets through year-end.

data.title: Dyne a new overweight at Cantor on platform for neuromuscular disorders
data.description:
• Cantor Fitzgerald has initiated Dyne Therapeutics (DYNE) at outperform, finding significant value in its FORCE platform to treat rare genetic muscle disease.
  • Analyst Eric Schmidt said that the company's lead asset, the Duchenne muscular dystrophy treatment zeleciment rostudirsen (z-rostudirsen), is likely to gain approval in January and become the preferred treatment for Duchenne patients amenable to Exon 51 skipping. It would compete against Sarepta Therapeutics' (SRPT) Exondys 51 (eteplirsen).
  • Schmidt projects $900M in annual sales by 2036.
  • "Its approval could also pave the way for other (preclinical) exon skippers that target mutations in exons 44, 45, 53 and 55," he wrote, adding that these assets could treat 24% of the Duchenne population.
  • z-basivarsen, for myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1), is Dyne's next most advanced candidate. Schmidt sees $3.7B in sales in 2036 based on 15% market share penetration.

data.title: United Rentals outlines 2026 revenue of $17.5B-$17.8B while raising adjusted EBITDA view to $7.975B-$8.125B
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: United Rentals (URI) Q2 2026

Management View

  • "2026 is on track to be a great year" and "our growth accelerated in the quarter," with CEO Matthew Flannery highlighting that the company is "pleased to raise guidance" as demand and large projects outpaced expectations (President, CEO & Director Matthew Flannery).
  • "Total revenue grew by 12% year-over-year to $4.4 billion" and "rental revenue grew by almost 13% to $3.8 billion," alongside "adjusted EBITDA...46.6%" margin and "adjusted EPS...$12.76" (President, CEO & Director Flannery).
  • Specialty was a major growth driver, with "exceptional rental revenue growth of 25% year-over-year, including 11 cold starts," and management said large projects broadened across "hospitals, airports and LNG terminals" while "data centers continue to be a source of growth" (President, CEO & Director Flannery).
  • The used market remained a key cash lever: "We sold $624 million of OEC at a 53% recovery rate" and management reiterated it is "on track to sell approximately $2.8 billion of fleet this year" (President, CEO & Director Flannery).
  • Higher fleet investment was positioned as demand-driven: "we spent nearly $2.1 billion on gross rental CapEx in the quarter" and "year-to-date, we spent $2.9 billion...The demand environment continues to outpace our original expectations" (President, CEO & Director Flannery).
  • CFO William Grace emphasized record-setting scale and confidence: "we set all-time second quarter records for total revenue, rental revenue, EBITDA and EPS" and "the increases to our 2026 guidance reflect our confidence" in demand and discipline in 2H26 (Executive VP & CFO William Grace).

Outlook

  • Updated full-year ranges were raised: "Total revenue is now expected in the range of $17.5 billion to $17.8 billion" and "we've also raised our adjusted EBITDA guidance by $300 million to a range of $7.975 billion to $8.125 billion" (Executive VP & CFO Grace).
  • Fleet and cash generation targets were maintained/expanded: "we've increased our gross CapEx guidance by $450 million to a range of $4.85 billion to $5.25 billion" and "we are reaffirming...free cash flow in the range of $2.15 billion to $2.45 billion" (Executive VP & CFO Grace).
  • Capital return intentions were unchanged: "we still intend to repurchase $1.5 billion of shares in 2026" and, with dividends, "this will return roughly $2 billion" (Executive VP & CFO Grace).
  • Versus Q1, guidance increases were larger and tied to project momentum: "demand outpaced even our revised expectations" and "the large projects drove this demand in the first half of the year, and we expect that will continue" (President, CEO & Director Flannery).
  • Analyst estimates were provided in the prompt, but the estimates file used a quarter format that did not match the required validation rules, so no estimates comparison was included.

Financial Results

  • Core growth and profitability were described as records: "rental revenue increased $434 million year-over-year or 12.7% to...over $3.8 billion" and "EBITDA increased $197 million to...just over $2 billion" (Executive VP & CFO Grace).
  • Used equipment monetization remained significant: "we sold $624 million of OEC...generating $330 million of proceeds" at "a 52.9% recovery rate" (Executive VP & CFO Grace).
  • One-time item disclosure: "Excluding the $49 million net benefit we realized this quarter from the sale of our scaffolding business" EBITDA still rose to a quarterly record (Executive VP & CFO Grace).
  • Balance sheet and credit positioning were highlighted: "Net leverage remained...at 1.8x" and S&P "rais[ed] our credit outlook to positive...with the potential to upgrade...to investment grade within the next 12 months" (Executive VP & CFO Grace).

Q&A

  • David Raso, Evercore ISI: asked about the swing to stronger 2H margins and whether higher utilization/CapEx implies visibility into 2027; President, CEO & Director Flannery said, "we certainly think these tailwinds...will carry into next year" and CFO Grace added, "we feel confident...to achieve our goal for the year, which is flat margins" (President, CEO & Director Flannery; Executive VP & CFO Grace).
  • Robert Wertheimer, Melius: asked about rate vs. CapEx and transportation-cost volatility; President, CEO & Director Flannery responded, "we need to get rate to further fund the business" and CFO Grace said delivery/labor/R&M were "ahead of the curve" even with repositioning variability (President, CEO & Director Flannery; Executive VP & CFO Grace).
  • Michael Feniger, BofA: asked about margin levers into 2027 and fuel/delivery; CFO Grace cited "20 or 30 basis points" fuel headwind and President, CEO & Director Flannery said delivery costs showed "positive absorption" despite fuel and hauling inflation (Executive VP & CFO Grace; President, CEO & Director Flannery).
  • Jerry Revich, Wells Fargo: asked about specialty growth areas and semis/data-center power; management said "all 7 parts of the specialty business" were growing and President, CEO & Director Flannery confirmed "semis...has grown and power continues to be a strong end market" (Executive VP & CFO Grace; President, CEO & Director Flannery).
  • Angel Castillo, Morgan Stanley: asked how potential investment-grade status affects capital allocation and about supplier tightness/2027 CapEx; CFO Grace said, "I don't think it really affects our capital allocation strategy at all" and President, CEO & Director Flannery added, "why not take the opportunity" while noting it is "way too early" to forecast next year’s CapEx (Executive VP & CFO Grace; President, CEO & Director Flannery).

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to neutral on cost predictability and margins, pressing on mix/ancillary, delivery/repositioning, and sustainability of 2H profitability, including references to "swing factors" and the "implied second half margins" setup (David Raso, Evercore ISI: Question).
  • Management’s tone was positive in prepared remarks and steady in Q&A, repeatedly tying guidance to demand visibility and execution, including "we certainly feel good about the demand" and "we feel confident" on maintaining "flat margins" (President, CEO & Director Flannery; Executive VP & CFO Grace).
  • Compared with Q1, management used more explicit confidence language around duration of tailwinds, while still avoiding 2027 numerical guidance: "we're not going to go as far as give '27 guidance" (President, CEO & Director Flannery).

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Q2 raised guidance more aggressively than Q1, with CFO Grace citing a "$500 million" revenue range increase versus prior guidance in Q2, compared with a smaller Q1 increase of "$100 million versus our initial guidance" (Executive VP & CFO Grace).
  • CapEx guidance moved higher again, expanding from Q1’s "$4.4 billion to $4.8 billion" to Q2’s "$4.85 billion to $5.25 billion," reflecting management’s view that it is "running at historically high time utilizations" (Executive VP & CFO Grace; President, CEO & Director Flannery).
  • Q2 analyst questions broadened from Q1’s heavy focus on delivery/repositioning mitigation to include investment-grade implications, supplier tightness, and how much 2027 demand visibility supports incremental fleet purchases (Angel Castillo, Morgan Stanley: Question; David Raso, Evercore ISI: Question).

Risks and Concerns

  • Management flagged forecasting volatility in ancillary and project-driven logistics: "it has been difficult to kind of forecast ancillary" and repositioning remains "the biggest point of variability" even as the team works on efficiency (Executive VP & CFO Grace).
  • Supplier responsiveness was described as constrained: "If we wanted another...$1 billion worth of fleet, we wouldn't be able to get it," with mitigation centered on "advanced purchase orders" and pulling orders forward where possible (President, CEO & Director Flannery).
  • Local markets were stable but not a growth engine: "our local customers...have grown low single digits" and management pointed to potential catalysts such as residential and small-business investment (President, CEO & Director Flannery).

Final Takeaway

Management presented Q2 as a demand-led acceleration driven by large projects and broad-based specialty momentum, pairing record quarterly results with a larger full-year guidance raise, higher fleet investment plans, and reaffirmed free cash flow and shareholder returns. Executives emphasized that elevated utilization and a deeper project pipeline underpin the added CapEx, while acknowledging persistent uncertainty around ancillary mix and delivery/repositioning variability and highlighting balance sheet flexibility alongside a potential investment-grade upgrade window.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Conventum - Alluvium Global Fund adds CPRT
data.description:
• Conventum—Alluvium Global Fund declined 1.4% in EUR terms during Q2 2026 amid broad market volatility.

  • During Q2 2026, the fund initiated a new position in Copart (NASDAQ: CPRT).

  • The fund did not disclose any complete portfolio exits in Q2 2026, though it reduced several existing positions.

  • Looking ahead, the fund continues to focus on buying high-quality businesses at attractive valuations while remaining cautious toward speculative areas of the market
  • Source.

data.title: PG&E’s data center project pipeline more than doubled in Q2
data.description:
PG&E (PCG) said Thursday its pipeline of proposed data center projects more than doubled to 12.7 GW at the end of Q2 from 5.1 GW at the end of Q1, as the data center buildout gains momentum in California.

However, the current makeup of the pipeline shows that while dozens of projects have advanced enough to sign an application and pay a study fee, far fewer are close to getting connected, according to a Bloomberg analysis.

The largest gain in Q2 came from projects that have applied and paid the initial fee: PG&E (PCG) now has 26 projects totaling 8.2 GW in that category, up from 1.7 GW in Q1, but only four projects comprising 490 MW of the pipeline have actual interconnection construction agreements, up from 140 MW.

PG&E (PCG) said each additional GW in its data center pipeline reduces customers' electricity bills by 1% or more, but results can "differ materially," depending on the pricing and rate design for large electricity users.

PG&E (PCG) trades down 1.5% after the company reported higher Q2 profit but flat revenue that missed analyst expectations.

Q2 profit rose to $761M, or $0.33/share, from $549M, or $0.24/share, in the year-earlier quarter, while revenue came in roughly flat Y/Y at $5.9B, as a small decline in the company's electric unit was offset by gains in its natural gas segment.

data.title: Huhtamäki Oyj 1H non-GAAP EPS of EUR 0.89, revenue of EUR 1.96B
Crypto exchange pioneering leveraged bitcoin derivatives set to close
data.description:
Faced with a crypto slump, HDR Global Trading Limited has decided to close BitMEX, the crypto exchange that invented perpetual swaps allowing as much as 100x leverage on bitcoin (BTC-USD).

BitMEX has stopped all new account registrations and will close the exchange on Sept. 23, 2026, 04:00:00 UTC, the company said in a blog post on Thursday.

"Following a strategic review of the business and the broader crypto industry, the board of HDR Global Trading Limited, owner and operator of BitMEX, has decided to close the exchange," it said.

The exchange started up in 2014 with the mission to "provide access to professional-grade crypto derivatives for everyone." The 100x leverage perpetual swap became the most traded product in the crypto industry and has been adopted by thousands of people and exchanges, BitMEX said.

BitMEX grew to be one of the largest crypto exchanges between 2017 and 2020. But in 2020, U.S. regulators accused the exchange and its founders of violating the Bank Secrecy Act by failing to implement sufficient anti-money-laundering controls, Bloomberg News said.

In 2021, the exchange agreed to pay $100M to resolve charges that it illegally operated a crypto trading platform and violated anti-money laundering laws. The week before the penalty was due, President Donald Trump pardoned  the company.

The firm created the perpetual swap derivative, which has no expiration date and eventually dominated crypto derivatives trading volume. Other crypto exchanges also created such perps, and the derivatives are now being applied to such markets as equities and oil.

Bitcoin (BTC-USD) slipped 1.6% to ~$64.9K on Thursday,  about 45% lower than a year ago and close to half its all-time high of $126K reached on Oct. 6, 2025.

data.title: West Pharmaceutical signals 10%-11% 2026 organic revenue growth and $8.85-$9.05 adjusted EPS amid leadership transition
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: West Pharmaceutical Services (WST) Q2 2026

Management view

  • "I am pleased to report strong second quarter results which exceeded our expectations on the top and bottom line." (Non-Independent Chair of the Board, President & CEO Eric Green) "Revenues of $872 million were up 13% organically, and adjusted EPS of $2.37, was up 29% as compared to prior year." (President & CEO Green)
  • "Given the robust outcome in the second quarter and the ongoing momentum in the business, we are raising our guidance for full year 2026." (President & CEO Green) "We now anticipate organic revenue growth to be 10% to 11% for the full year and adjusted EPS to be in the range of $8.85 to $9.05." (President & CEO Green)
  • "Our Proprietary Products segment delivered 16% organic growth, led by the Biologics market group, which was up 29% organic." (President & CEO Green) "The largest contributor continues to be our HVP Components business, which increased by 18% on an organic basis." (President & CEO Green)
  • "On July 1, we completed the sale and transfer of the manufacturing and supply rights for SmartDose 3.5mL On-Body Delivery System and associated facilities as planned." (President & CEO Green) "Finally, I'm very pleased that we recently announced the renewal of our existing agreements with our strategic partner, Daikyo." (President & CEO Green)
  • "As Eric mentioned, we had a strong second quarter as revenues of $872 million increased 13.8% on a reported basis and grew 12.7% organically, exceeding our expectations overall." (Senior VP & CFO Robert McMahon) "Price contributed 4 percentage points of that growth in the quarter." (CFO McMahon)
  • "As I pass the torch of leadership and welcome Michel Lagarde on August 31." (President & CEO Green)

Outlook

  • "For the year, we now anticipate revenue to be in the range of $3.345 billion to $3.380 billion, growing 10% to 11% organically which is up from 7% to 9% previously." (Senior VP & CFO Robert McMahon) "This results in adjusted earnings per share to be between $8.85 to $9.05 for the year." (CFO McMahon)
  • "Our updated guidance now assumes a 1 percentage point tailwind from currency, down from our prior guidance assumption of roughly a 2 percentage point tailwind." (CFO McMahon) "From a margin perspective, while we are experiencing some inflationary pressure from higher oil and commodity prices, we are working hard to offset those costs through various means and do not see a change from our previous guidance." (CFO McMahon)
  • "We expect revenue to be in the range of $820 million to $835 million." (CFO McMahon) "And we expect third quarter adjusted diluted earnings per share in the range of $2.14 to $2.24." (CFO McMahon)
  • Prior-quarter guidance was raised in Q1 to "7% to 9%" organic growth and "$8.40 to $8.75" adjusted EPS; Q2 raised it again to "10% to 11%" and "$8.85 to $9.05." (Senior VP & CFO Robert McMahon; Non-Independent Chair of the Board, President & CEO Eric Green)

Financial results

  • "Total company gross margin was 37.7% in the quarter, up 200 basis points year-over-year." (Senior VP & CFO Robert McMahon) "Adjusted operating margins of 22.6% were up 230 basis points compared to the prior year." (CFO McMahon)
  • "In the quarter, we delivered operating cash flow of $124 million." (CFO McMahon) "Capital expenditures were $43 million." (CFO McMahon) "In Q2, we repurchased just over 0.5 million shares for $157 million and paid out $16 million in dividends." (CFO McMahon)
  • "Within proprietary, our HVP Components business... delivering $424 million in revenue and growing 18.4% organically." (CFO McMahon) "HVP Delivery Devices revenues were $131 million." (CFO McMahon) "Standard Products, revenues of $167 million." (CFO McMahon) "West Vantage delivered $150 million in revenue." (CFO McMahon)
  • "Adding it all up, Q2 adjusted earnings per share were $2.37... and $0.28 above the midpoint of guidance we gave on the last earnings call." (CFO McMahon)

Q&A

  • Michael Ryskin, BofA Securities: asked what is driving non-GLP-1 HVP Components strength and how sustainable it is; President & CEO Green said it came down to "3 areas" including "Biologics and Biosimilar space," "Annex 1 and other HVP-related upgrades," and "operational excellence," adding, "We have just shy of 800 total projects in hand and that's up 50% from the same time period last year." (Non-Independent Chair of the Board, President & CEO Eric Green)
  • Michael Ryskin, BofA Securities: questioned conservatism in the second-half outlook; Senior VP & CFO McMahon replied, "we're taking kind of one quarter at a time and kind of a prudent approach" and "don't read too much into it." (CFO McMahon)
  • Paul Knight, KeyBanc: asked about capacity utilization and expansions at Eschweiler, Grand Rapids, and Dublin; President & CEO Green said Eschweiler productivity and throughput improved "double-digit" in the first half, Grand Rapids was "close" to expected throughput levels, and Dublin was "still in the ramping phase" with more capacity available. (President & CEO Green)
  • Patrick Donnelly, Citi: asked about margins and pricing; Senior VP & CFO McMahon said price was "4 points of the growth in Q2" and "we're saying over 200 basis points for the full year" operating margin expansion, while noting "inflationary pressure" from oil and commodities. (CFO McMahon)
  • Matthew Larew, William Blair: asked what is most interesting in delivery devices post-SmartDose 3.5; President & CEO Green pointed to "SelfDose," "Crystal Zenith," and "administration systems," adding, "when you take the SmartDose 3.5 out the margin profile is attractive for us going forward." (President & CEO Green)
  • Daniel Markowitz, Evercore ISI: asked whether GLP-1 second-half assumptions reflect demand; Senior VP & CFO McMahon said, "more prudent than anything else" and "We're not seeing anything in the marketplace," and characterized ex-U.S. generics as "really a forward-looking opportunity" for "'27 and the future years." (CFO McMahon)
  • Michael Polark, Wolfe Research: questioned the bridge between disclosed GLP-1 mix and management’s “high teens” growth; Senior VP & CFO McMahon said, "it is high teens," and offered to follow up on modeling, adding, "we feel very good about the continued performance of our GLP-1 portfolio." (CFO McMahon)

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts were slightly positive in tone on results while pressing on durability and guide conservatism, including "how sustainable you think that is" and "Any other moving pieces in the second half we should be wary of." (Michael Ryskin, BofA Securities)
  • Management tone was positive in prepared remarks and more measured in guidance framing during Q&A, including "we remain prudent with our forecasting" and "don't read too much into it." (Senior VP & CFO Robert McMahon)
  • Versus Q1, management repeated a similar "prudent" stance but paired it with a second consecutive full-year raise, and analysts shifted more heavily into dissecting mix shift (Annex 1 and biologics), GLP-1 trajectory, and post-SmartDose margin implications. (Senior VP & CFO Robert McMahon; Non-Independent Chair of the Board, President & CEO Eric Green)

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Guidance language moved from Q1’s "back to our long-term construct of 7% to 9%" organic growth to Q2’s "10% to 11%," with full-year adjusted EPS raised from "$8.40 to $8.75" to "$8.85 to $9.05." (President & CEO Green)
  • Operational narrative shifted from Q1’s emphasis on scaling and supply-demand balance to Q2’s focus on recovery from "the cyber incident" and the revenue timing impacts in West Vantage. (President & CEO Green; Senior VP & CFO Robert McMahon)
  • Leadership discussion became explicit in Q2 with the CEO’s transition timeline: "welcome Michel Lagarde on August 31." (President & CEO Green)

Risks and concerns

  • "West Vantage was up 1% organically... slightly below our expectations due to the impact of the cyber incident." (Non-Independent Chair of the Board, President & CEO Eric Green) "We estimate this was a mid-single-digit impact to growth in the quarter and is expected to be made up in the remainder of the year." (Senior VP & CFO Robert McMahon)
  • "West Vantage is expected to decline as Q3 represents the first quarter of the CGM contract exiting." (Senior VP & CFO Robert McMahon) "We expect Q3 to be the trough for West Vantage." (CFO McMahon)
  • "While we are experiencing some inflationary pressure from higher oil and commodity prices, we are working hard to offset those costs." (Senior VP & CFO Robert McMahon)

Final takeaway

Management framed Q2 as a broad-based beat that drove another full-year raise, with HVP Components (including biologics and Annex 1 upgrades) and delivery devices highlighted as key drivers. The company emphasized that West Vantage performance and margins were temporarily pressured by the cyber incident and that Q3 is expected to be the trough as the CGM contract exit begins, while reiterating pricing actions, mix benefits, and the operational recovery narrative. The call also formalized an imminent CEO transition, with Eric Green stating he will "welcome Michel Lagarde on August 31."

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Chinese titanium suppliers target Western aerospace buyers at Farnborough
data.description:
Chinese titanium producers used this week's Farnborough Airshow to expand their presence with Western aerospace manufacturers, seeking to capitalize on tight global supplies of the metal despite U.S. tariffs and broader trade tensions, Reuters reported Thursday.

For investors, the strong turnout highlighted how supply chain pressures continue to reshape the aerospace industry. Titanium remains a critical material for aircraft production, and shortages caused by the pandemic, sanctions on Russia and trade restrictions have created opportunities for alternative suppliers even as geopolitical risks persist.

Nearly two dozen Chinese companies, including HST Titanium, Jinda Titanium and Baoji YongshengTai, exhibited products ranging from titanium sponge and ingots to aerospace components, marking one of China's largest-ever presences at the air show.

Industry participants said Chinese suppliers are increasingly looking overseas as competition intensifies in their domestic market, which is led by state-backed aircraft manufacturer COMAC.

Although U.S. tariffs initially disrupted exports, executives said demand from overseas customers remains healthy because global titanium supplies are limited. Several suppliers also said their materials are being used in Airbus aircraft, though Airbus did not comment.

Analysts said the large Chinese presence at Farnborough reflects a broader effort to build relationships with aerospace companies in North America and Europe while expanding China's role in the global aviation supply chain.

COMAC also had a prominent display at the event as it seeks to challenge Boeing Co. (BA) and Airbus in commercial aviation. Analysts said the Chinese manufacturer's long-term success will depend not only on producing competitive aircraft but also on building the global maintenance, spare-parts and support networks that airlines expect.

data.title: Israel AI czar lobbies TSMC, Intel, Samsung to build chip manufacturing hub: report
data.description:
Israel's head of the National Artificial Intelligence Directorate, Erez Askal, has recently lobbied chip manufacturers to develop a hub for producing semiconductors based on a 2nm or less process, according to Bloomberg.    

Askal, a former brigadier general in the Israeli military, has reached out to Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM), Samsung (SSNLF) and Intel (INTC) as he works to build out the nation's chipmaking and AI infrastructure capabilities, the report said. He is also pushing for the development of a data center boasting 100,000 GPUs within the next five years. His efforts are complicated by Israel's ongoing war with Hamas stemming from the October 7, 2023, terrorist attack and a constrained electrical grid.

"I would view it as a very big failure if I don't get an advanced fab here from one of the companies," Askal said, according to the report.

Intel started to build its $25B "Fab 38" plant in Kiryat Gat, Israel, back in 2023. However, the project has since been paused due to changing market dynamics. Intel has about 9,000 employees based in Israel.

At the same time, some of Israel's neighbors are busy building out AI infrastructure, including the United Arab Emirates and Saudi Arabia. 

"During the three years of war, no data centers in Israel were damaged, and they operated fully," Askal said in the report. "We see what's happening with our neighbors. We have a good relationship with them and are trying to help so that it won't happen there."

What's more, Israel's Electricity Authority announced on Monday it was suspending the processing of new data center grid connection requests for the next 140 days after applications reached 27 GW. To put that into perspective, Israel's all-time peak load reached 17 GW during a heat wave in 2025. It averages about 9 GW.
